elements.sent-2411-001: We present~KV-Tandem, a modular architecture for building LSM-based storage engines on top of simple, non-ordered persistent key-value stores (KVSs).
elements.sent-2411-002: KV-Tandem enables advanced functionalities such as range queries and snapshot reads, while maintaining the native KVS performance for random reads and writes.
elements.sent-2411-003: Its modular design offers better performance trade-offs compared to previous KV-separation solutions, which struggle to decompose the monolithic LSM structure.
elements.sent-2411-004: Central to KV-Tandem is~LSM bypass -- a novel algorithm that offers a fast path to basic operations while ensuring the correctness of advanced APIs.
elements.sent-2411-005: We implement KV-Tandem in XDP-Rocks, a RocksDB-compatible storage engine that leverages the XDP KVS and incorporates practical design optimizations for real-world deployment.
elements.sent-2411-006: Through extensive microbenchmark and system-level comparisons, we demonstrate that XDP-Rocks achieves 3x to 4x performance improvements over RocksDB across various workloads.
elements.sent-2411-007: XDP-Rocks is already deployed in production, delivering significant operator cost savings consistent with these performance gains.
elements.sent-2411-008: Popular ordered key-value storage engines such as RocksDB and its siblings~\cite{RocksDB, Terarkdb, BlobDB, Titan, Pebble provide access to values associated with unique keys.}
elements.sent-2411-009: This includes not only point put/get queries of individual keys, but also snapshots (allowing users to read multiple values at a common point in time) and iterators.
elements.sent-2411-010: Such state-of-the-art systems are typically organized as log-structured merge (LSM) trees~\cite{LSM96 , LSMs for short, which facilitate ordered operations such as range queries.}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, despite their advantages for ordered operations, LSM-based systems incur significant trade-offs in write, read, and space efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-012: In a nutshell, LSMs buffer written data in memory, periodically flushing it to disk as sorted files and merging those files into larger ones through a background compaction process.
elements.sent-2411-013: Yet this multi-file structure introduces three key inefficiencies: write amplification (WA), as data is rewritten (copied to a different file) multiple times; read amplification (RA), due to searches across multiple files; and space amplification (SA), as storage is used inefficiently until compaction removes stale entries.
elements.sent-2411-014: These three performance metrics are at inherent tension with each other~\cite{LuoCarey , and hinder the performance of existing systems, particularly in write-heavy workloads, leading to high tail latency, limited scalability, or significant space consumption~\cite{DayanI18, DayanWDPBT22 .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Techniques like key-value separation~\cite{wiscKey are useful for mitigating this difficulty, but, as of today, suffer from a number of shortcomings that preclude their widespread deployment, as we explain below.}
elements.sent-2411-016: In contrast, an unordered key-value store (KVS), (e.g., a SNIA-defined KVSSD~\cite{KVSSD ) provides efficient point put/get access without supporting complex queries like snapshots or range scans.}
elements.sent-2411-017: This allows for lightweight solutions based on log-structured storage indexed by an in-memory hashtable, e.g., Bitcask~\cite{Bitcask and Pliops's Extreme Data Processor (XDP)~\cite{DayanI18 , which achieve higher throughput and lower WA.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, unordered KVSs lack support for ordered operations (unless all the data can be indexed in memory).. \remove{ .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Unlike an ordered storage engine, a KVS offers only point put/get access.
elements.sent-2411-020: Forgoing support for snapshots and range queries allows for lightweight solutions based on log-structured storage indexed by an in-memory hashtable, e.g., Bitcask~\cite{Bitcask .}
elements.sent-2411-021: A state-of-the-art KVS, e.g., the Pliops Extreme Data Processor (XDP)~\cite{DayanI18 , can serve random reads with a single I/O and offers a write throughput close the the underlying storage device's.}
elements.sent-2411-022: For comparison, a highly optimized LSM entails double-digit WA, thereby exploiting only a small percentage of the write bandwidth~\cite{LuoCarey .}
elements.sent-2411-023: On the negative side, unordered KVSs do not support range queries (except in DRAM-bounded data stores where all the data can be indexed in memory~\cite{ForestDB ).}
elements.sent-2411-024: While common NoSQL workloads use range queries much less frequently than random access~\cite{SrinivasanBCSGI16 , applications do need to use them from time to time, e.g., for sorted set listings~\cite{RedisAPI or administrative tasks like whole-database backup~\cite{RoF .}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The aim of this work is to get the best of both worlds, namely, to serve point gets and puts at (almost) maximum KVS speed with minimal SA, and to offer alongside them the full functionality of RocksDB-like LSM storage engines.
elements.sent-2411-026: We target common NoSQL workloads, which use range queries much less frequently than random access~\cite{SrinivasanBCSGI16 , but do use them from time to time, e.g., for sorted set listings~\cite{RedisAPI or administrative tasks like whole-database backup~\cite{RoF .}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Such workloads are common among consumer Internet application providers, e.g., social networks~\cite{CaoDVD20 , including some of our customers.}
elements.sent-2411-028: In this work we identify and exploit KVS as a fundamental building block for a feature-rich high-throughput ordered storage engine.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our design, KV-Tandem, couples two distinct paradigms.
elements.sent-2411-030: Its unordered value storage is a KVS, whereas its ordered key storage is an LSM.
elements.sent-2411-031: A novel LSM bypass mechanism allows point queries to directly access the KVS without going through the LSM.
elements.sent-2411-032: The LSM itself is also stored as a collection of KV-pairs in the same KVS, simplifying storage management.
elements.sent-2411-033: The KV-Tandem architecture is depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:XDP-Rocks .}
elements.sent-2411-034: This design is generic and agnostic to the KVS's implementation (be it in software or hardware, operating on directly-attached or remote storage).
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.5\columnwidth]{KV-Tandem-2.jpg} \caption{KV-Tandem high-level architecture: providing a storage engine API (RocksDB-compatible) atop a KVS. Values are embedded in the KVS directly, while their keys are embedded indirectly through the LSM tree. The LSM bypass offers a fast-path for point queries, while iterators and snapshots go through the LSM. The KVS GC and the LSM compaction are independent.} \label{fig:XDP-Rocks}
elements.sent-2411-035: Restricting the LSM to store keys (without values) leverages the key-value separation approach pioneered in WiscKey~\cite{wiscKey , which eliminates the bulk of the WA cost by avoiding repeated compaction of large values.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Since keys are often much smaller than values (see, e.g., a survey of Meta workloads~\cite{CaoDVD20 ), this approach greatly reduces compaction overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-037: A number of works, including production storage engines~\cite{Terarkdb, Titan, BlobDB , have adopted and enhanced key-value separation in different ways.}
elements.sent-2411-038: The idea is to store values in log files, with the LSM holding physical pointers into these log files.
elements.sent-2411-039: However, these systems only achieve competitive LSM read performance when all keys fit in memory~\cite{wiscKey .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Otherwise, queries suffer from additional indirection I/Os due to first searching the LSM for a given key and then chasing pointers to the log files.
elements.sent-2411-041: They also typically induce redundant LSM accesses for value-log garbage collection (GC), hampering write throughput~\cite{wiscKey .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Finally and most problematically, some of these solutions~\cite{BlobDB,Terarkdb incur high (unbounded) SA under write-intensive workloads, caused by tight coupling between the GC and the LSM's compaction.}
elements.sent-2411-043: We, instead, treat unordered key-value storage as a first-class citizen, and embody it through a state-of-the-art KVS.
elements.sent-2411-044: The latter handles search, update, and GC internally through scalable mechanisms tailored to the simple KVS API.
elements.sent-2411-045: We complement the KVS with an LSM, which in a sense serves as a secondary index for the data in the KVS.
elements.sent-2411-046: The LSM is needed for two purposes: lexicographic key order and multi-version concurrency control (MVCC)~\cite{WeikumVossen2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: The former enables range queries, and the latter supports transactionally consistent snapshot reads that observe the values of multiple keys at a common point in time.
elements.sent-2411-048: Combining the KVS and LSM paradigms introduces a number of challenges, which we address in this work.
elements.sent-2411-049: Most importantly, we have to circumvent the indirection penalty of searching through the LSM when it is not required.
elements.sent-2411-050: To forgo this cost, we introduce the LSM bypass mechanism, which skips the LSM search altogether whenever the requested key has a single version.
elements.sent-2411-051: The main difficulty is to detect such situations quickly and to transition safely between the multiple-version and single-version scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-052: Much of the KV-Tandem algorithm is dedicated to handling this difficulty through a novel use of Bloom filters and additional mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-053: Note that the LSM bypass approach greatly improves point get/put performance (by removing a level of indirection), but is disadvantageous for range scans, as the KVS does not support ordered iteration.
elements.sent-2411-054: This tradeoff is justified by our target workload, which uses range scans sparingly.
elements.sent-2411-055: We materialize the KV-Tandem architecture in~XDP-Rocks -- a full-fledged RocksDB-compliant storage engine, leveraging Pliops's XDP~\cite{Dayan-XDP for the unordered KVS.}
elements.sent-2411-056: XDP is hardware-accelerated to alleviate CPU bottlenecks related to key lookup, compression, GC, etc.; these features are orthogonal to KV-Tandem's design.
elements.sent-2411-057: The crucial aspect of the underlying KVS is the speed of random reads, which in XDP is close to the raw I/O speed thanks to the extremely lean memory footprint of its hashmap index that allows it to fit in DRAM even for large datasets.
elements.sent-2411-058: XDP-Rocks is deployed by a number of commercial customers, which switched away from RocksDB to reduce costs and upgrade SLAs.
elements.sent-2411-059: A notable large-scale production deployment is at a hyperscaler video-sharing network.
elements.sent-2411-060: XDP-Rocks powers its internal distributed key-value database with Redis API~\cite{RedisAPI , which supports dozens of consumer applications.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Transitioning to XDP-Rocks allowed that operator to reduce its server fleet almost three-fold.
elements.sent-2411-062: Building XDP-Rocks required solving a number of nontrivial practical issues that arise in the KV-Tandem architecture.
elements.sent-2411-063: This includes shared storage management for LSM and KVS data, in-memory data caching, high-speed backup, and additional support for RocksDB's advanced APIs.
elements.sent-2411-064: Some of these challenges have been raised by industry users to enable transition from RocksDB.
elements.sent-2411-065: We provide an extensive evaluation of XDP-Rocks.
elements.sent-2411-066: We first compare it to BlobDB~\cite{BlobDB , a state-of-the-art storage engine that implements WiscKey-style KV-separation in the RocksDB architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-067: We show that whereas BlobDB quickly runs out of space under write-heavy workloads due to its large SA, XDP-Rocks's SA is close to one.
elements.sent-2411-068: This allows us to support much larger datasets.
elements.sent-2411-069: We then compare XDP-Rocks to RocksDB in a broad set of RocksDB dbbench microbenchmarks~\cite{RocksDB at production scale.}
elements.sent-2411-070: XDP-Rocks exhibits higher average- and worst-case performance versus RocksDB in random writes, random reads, and combinations thereof.
elements.sent-2411-071: For example, we show a 3.5x throughput increase versus RocksDB under uniform random writes and 3.8x increase under mixed reads and writes.
elements.sent-2411-072: As expected, XDP-Rocks falls short of RocksDB in scan performance, but this can be mitigated through internal parallelism, to reduce the gap to only 20\% for a single scan.
elements.sent-2411-073: We also evaluate XDP-Rocks at the system level, by using it instead of RocksDB as the storage engine of the popular Kvrocks distributed NoSQL database~\cite{KVRocks .}
elements.sent-2411-074: The experiments show high performance gains that correlate with the microbenchmarks -- e.g., a 20.5x throughput increase over a big dataset under a mixed read-write workload.
elements.sent-2411-075: Indeed, we do try to read from the KVS even if the key is not in the LSM.

elements.sent-2411-078: This paper makes the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-079: We introduce a novel storage paradigm combining KVS and LSM with a new approach to KV-separation; background on the existing paradigms we leverage is given in Section~\ref{sec:background .}
elements.sent-2411-080: In Section~\ref{sec:kv-tandem , we present the KV-Tandem ordered storage algorithm realizing this paradigm.}
elements.sent-2411-081: We implement XDP-Rocks, an ordered storage engine based on KV-Tandem, in Section~\ref{sec:implementation .}
elements.sent-2411-082: In Section~\ref{sec:results we evaluate XDP-Rocks through microbenchmarks and system-level tests.}
elements.sent-2411-083: We compare it with both RocksDB (performance baseline) and XDP (performance upper-bound).
elements.sent-2411-084: Section~\ref{sec:discussion discusses KV-Tandem's advantages and limitations and outlines future work.}
elements.sent-2411-085: We now provide some essential background for this work.
elements.sent-2411-086: Our goal is to implement the API of a rich ordered storage engine, e.g., RocksDB~\cite{RocksDB described in Section~\ref{sec:API .}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Our design extends the LSM-tree paradigm, which we discuss in Section~\ref{sec:lsm .}
elements.sent-2411-088: It shares some ideas with prior work on KV-separation but also differs from it profoundly, as discussed in Section~\ref{sec:kv-sep .}
elements.sent-2411-089: Storage engines~\cite{LevelDB, RocksDB, Pebble, WiredTiger organize KV pairs in containers called databases~\cite{RocksDB (also known as tablets~\cite{ChangDGHWBCFG08 and collections~\cite{WiredTiger ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: A database is a unit of storage management (space allocation, security, replication, etc.) and a gateway to all KV APIs.
elements.sent-2411-091: Most storage engines provide the following basic APIs: random (point) read -- \code{get(k) , range read -- \code{iterate(from\_k,to\_k) , and random-write -- \code{put(k,v) and \code{delete(k) .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: The \code{iterate API allows traversing either the keys or the KV pairs within a range, in either ascending or descending order.}
elements.sent-2411-093: The put API stands for either an insert of a new KV pair or a value update for an existing key.
elements.sent-2411-094: It is worth noting that puts and deletes do not guarantee immediate durability (the update might not persist through crash failures) because they typically return after writing in memory without waiting for a disk flush.
elements.sent-2411-095: Common ordered storage engines (e.g.,~\cite{RocksDB, WiredTiger ) also provide higher-level transaction APIs that facilitate ACID semantics for multiple reads and writes.}
elements.sent-2411-096: For performance reasons they typically favor the snapshot isolation consistency model, which in particular guarantees that all reads pertain to the same point-in-time view of the dataset, called snapshot.
elements.sent-2411-097: They thus offer APIs for snapshot creation and for reading in it -- \code{get@sn(k,sn) and \code{iterate@sn(from\_k,to\_k,sn) .}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Storage engines also provide more eclectic APIs, e.g., range delete, checkpoint (persistent snapshot), compaction filter (auto-expiration), and others in RocksDB~\cite{RocksDB .}
elements.sent-2411-099: We do not focus on these functions here unless supporting them projects on KV-Tandem.
elements.sent-2411-100: KV-Tandem employs the popular LSM data structure~\cite{LSM96 , which is used by many production ordered storage engines~\cite{LevelDB, Pebble, RocksDB, Speedb .}}
elements.sent-2411-101: This section is an essential summary of LSM structure for understanding our KV-Tandem solution.
elements.sent-2411-102: LSMs optimize write I/O by transforming small random writes to I/O-friendly big sequential writes, as follows.
elements.sent-2411-103: All incoming updates are staged in a sorted memory buffer called memtable, typically 64-128 MB big~\cite{RocksDB .}
elements.sent-2411-104: Whenever memtable reaches capacity, it is flushed into storage as a sorted file called a sorted string table (SST).
elements.sent-2411-105: The SST file becomes immutable and searchable at the end of flush.
elements.sent-2411-106: SST files are later merged through a background compaction process, which also removes obsolete (over-written or deleted) data.
elements.sent-2411-108: The topmost level, L0, is filled by flushes.
elements.sent-2411-109: Every lower level consists of a collection (also called run) of SST files with disjoint ranges.
elements.sent-2411-110: Whenever a level reaches capacity, some SST file is picked to be merge-sorted with intersecting SST files at the next (lower and larger) level(s).
elements.sent-2411-111: The LSM components (memtable and SST files) store $\langle$\code{key , \code{sn , \code{value}$\rangle$ triples, each capturing a distinct version of \code{key .}}}
elements.sent-2411-112: The \code{key}\ and \code{value}\ are application-provided, whereas \code{sn is a logical timestamp (sequence number) generated by a global logical clock (counter) that is promoted upon every update.}
elements.sent-2411-113: A snapshot handle \code{S is the \code{sn at the time of that snapshot's creation.}}
elements.sent-2411-114: A simple read of key \code{k returns its last written version.}
elements.sent-2411-115: A read from snapshot \code{S returns the value of the latest version of \code{k}\ whose sequence number precedes \code{S .}}
elements.sent-2411-116: Each SST file is sorted by $\langle$\code{key , \code{sn}$\rangle$ to provide logarithmic-time binary search for random reads and sequential access in range reads.}
elements.sent-2411-117: LSMs typically maintain a range-to-block index per SST file in order to restrict key searches to one block per SST file.
elements.sent-2411-118: The index is concise and is cached in RAM with high priority.
elements.sent-2411-119: Since the ranges of SST files in different levels overlap, gets need to search all LSM levels, from new to old.
elements.sent-2411-120: To mitigate the RA cost, LSMs maintain, per SST file, a Bloom filter~\cite{ChangDGHWBCFG08 -- a succinct statistical summary of the keys in that file.}
elements.sent-2411-121: Bloom filters have no false negatives and low-probability false positives (infrequently return true for a non-existing key).
elements.sent-2411-122: Thus, the actual file search (and I/O) can be skipped in most cases when the key is not present in the file.
elements.sent-2411-123: Bloom filters, like SST indexes, are cached with high priority.
elements.sent-2411-124: Note that the Bloom filter optimization only applies to random reads.
elements.sent-2411-125: Range-read queries merge the range content from multiple SST files.
elements.sent-2411-126: To facilitate data durability between the insertion into memtable and the flush, every update is persisted to a write-ahead log (WAL) within a short time of its occurance.
elements.sent-2411-127: The WAL is not searchable; its primary use is during recovery from crash failures.
elements.sent-2411-128: Once memtable is persisted in the form of an SST file, the WAL entries pertaining to that data may be recycled.
elements.sent-2411-129: Deletions are implemented by storing a special $\langle\code{k , \code{sn , \bot\rangle$ version, called tombstone, which designates a deletion of key \code{k at time \code{sn .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: A $\langle\code{from\_k , \code{to\_k , \code{sn , \bot\rangle$ tombstone designates a deletion of a whole range.}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: A get that retrieves a tombstone, either single-key or range, returns no result.
elements.sent-2411-132: Compactions that encounter tombstones remove the corresponding deleted entries from the LSM.
elements.sent-2411-133: A compaction that spans the lowest LSM level also eliminates the tombstone itself (as long as no earlier version survives); at higher levels, the tombstone is kept in the SST file.
elements.sent-2411-134: Every flush creates a new run (SST file).
elements.sent-2411-135: LSM trees bound the number of runs (and consequently, the worst-case RA) through a background process named compaction, which merges small runs into bigger ones.
elements.sent-2411-136: One other function of compaction is eliminating the old key versions that are no more accessible to queries, thereby bounding the SA too.
elements.sent-2411-137: Note that historical versions may not be removed if they may be required by some active snapshot read.
elements.sent-2411-138: Once a compaction completes, a new run (one or more immutable SSTs) becomes part of the LSM, and the input SSTs are discarded.
elements.sent-2411-139: The sorted runs are organized in levels (one or more per level).
elements.sent-2411-140: The topmost level, named L0, is filled by flushes.
elements.sent-2411-141: A compaction is triggered when some level fills up.
elements.sent-2411-142: It may merge any number of contiguous levels into the next-lower level (e.g., levels L3--L5 into level L6).
elements.sent-2411-143: Level capacities grow exponentially, thereby most of the data sinks to the bottommost levels.
elements.sent-2411-145: While compactions reduce the RA and SA metrics, they also increase the WA, as the data is copied across levels multiple times.
elements.sent-2411-146: For large datasets, the WA can exceed 50x, leading the system to utilize less than 2\% of the storage device's bandwidth~\cite{LuoCarey .}
elements.sent-2411-147: Furthermore, compactions cause unpredictable performance fluctuations~\cite{GiladBBGHKMS20, LuoC19a .}
elements.sent-2411-148: A host of research studied different compaction policies to navigate tradeoffs in the LSM performance space (see survey in~\cite{AthanassoulisIS23 ), but the tradeoffs between the metrics are inherent to the LSM approach.}
elements.sent-2411-149: To overcome this conundrum, the seminal WiscKey paper~\cite{wiscKey suggested to store data values separately from the LSM to avoid repeatedly compacting them and thereby reduce WA.}
elements.sent-2411-150: WiscKey stores the values in external files called value-logs (v-logs) and uses an LSM to keep $\langle$\code{key , \code{sn , \code{loc}$\rangle$ triples, where \code{loc is the value location (v-log id and offset).}}}
elements.sent-2411-151: Consequently, only the keys and their concise metadata get re-written by compactions, rather than the whole dataset.
elements.sent-2411-152: In many KV-storage applications, the values are an order of magnitude bigger than the keys~\cite{CaoDVD20, CooperSTRS10 , so this approach drastically reduces WA.}
elements.sent-2411-153: The KV-separated LSM is essentially a secondary index into the v-log storage.
elements.sent-2411-154: Every read starts from searching the LSM for the value reference and then retrieves the value from a v-log file.
elements.sent-2411-155: This two-hop access scheme increases RA (affecting read latency and throughput) unless the whole (albeit reduced) LSM tree fits into memory.
elements.sent-2411-156: In reality, a system might not have that much RAM -- some systems have a RAM-to-storage ratio of 2\% and below~\cite{ClickhouseSizing , meaning that the LSM-tree cannot be fully cached.}
elements.sent-2411-157: KV-Tandem eliminates this constraint through LSM bypass, which skips the LSM search altogether.
elements.sent-2411-158: Another challenge introduced by KV-separation is garbage collecting obsolete values from the value log.
elements.sent-2411-159: Note that LSM stores physical v-log addresses, so value relocation by GC incurs I/O overhead.
elements.sent-2411-160: WiscKey's GC accesses the LSM twice for each relocated value, first to query whether the value is still valid and then to update the address mapping.
elements.sent-2411-161: This mechanism manifests in significant write rate loss~\cite{wiscKey .}
elements.sent-2411-162: HashKV~\cite{Chan18 improves upon WiscKey's GC by partitioning the v-log by keys, which allows them to check validity within a v-log partition without an LSM query.}
elements.sent-2411-163: However, it increases the SA due to fragmentation.
elements.sent-2411-164: Production KV-separated LSMs, e.g., BlobDB~\cite{BlobDB and TerarkDB~\cite{Terarkdb , have lazy GC: they track value invalidations at compaction time and discard a v-log file as a whole once all its values become invalid.}}
elements.sent-2411-165: This design implies unbounded SA under heavy writes (as we show in Section~\ref{sec:results ), which is impractical in many cases.}
elements.sent-2411-166: KV-Tandem's use of a KVS decouples the values' GC from the LSM and imposes no constraints on physical GC.
elements.sent-2411-167: KV-separation is not beneficial for small values, as it fails to improve WA significantly while putting extra pressure on RA and SA.
elements.sent-2411-168: Practical systems take a hybrid approach, storing large values in v-logs while embedding the rest in the LSM.
elements.sent-2411-169: For example, BlobDB~\cite{BlobDB recommends to apply KV-separation only to blobs 1KB and above.}
elements.sent-2411-170: In XDP-Rocks, we also support this hybrid approach, namely, embed small values in the LSM.
elements.sent-2411-171: Because XDP-Rocks uses an optimized KVS with very low per-value overhead, it reduces the separation threshold to 128--256B.
elements.sent-2411-172: They retrieve every value separately, thus not only failing to exploit sequential I/O but also introducing RA to the random I/O, especially for sub-4-KB values.
elements.sent-2411-173: A host of research suggested hybrid designs to mitigate that gap through limited locality of storage (DiffKV~\cite{LiLiu21 ), moving the values in and out the LSM (Parallax~\cite{XanthakisSBPB21 ), etc. KV-Tandem is complementary to that work, since it applies to all values stored outside the LSM.}}
elements.sent-2411-174: We now present KV-Tandem, a new KV storage paradigm that combines KVS with an LSM index and bypasses the latter in the common case.
elements.sent-2411-175: KV-Tandem stores data in the KVS in two distinct key-encoding modes, direct and versioned, which we introduce in Section~\ref{ssec:basic .}
elements.sent-2411-176: Section~\ref{sec:read-write-alg then presents the algorithm's basic read and write paths.}
elements.sent-2411-177: The combination of independent KVS and LSM engines introduces challenges in recovering from crashes; we address them in Section~\ref{sec:recovery .}
elements.sent-2411-178: Our approach is founded on the observation that most keys in an ordered KV storage engine are associated with a single value (version).
elements.sent-2411-179: Multiple versions need to be kept only if a key is overwritten in the course of an ongoing snapshot, whereas common workloads of ordered KV storage engines, e.g., Redis-on-Flash~\cite{RoF , apply snapshots only in specific scenarios such as backup.}
elements.sent-2411-180: To optimize for the common single-version case while maintaining the ability to support multi-versioning, we use two distinct key encoding schemes in KVS (storage modes).
elements.sent-2411-181: Keys that have a single version are (typically) stored in direct mode in the KVS, and can be accessed without the LSM.
elements.sent-2411-182: Other keys are stored in versioned mode, and their access requires indirection via the LSM.
elements.sent-2411-183: These modes are transparent to the KVS, which handles all keys identically.
elements.sent-2411-184: In direct mode, we keep KV-pairs in the KVS directly under their application-provided keys.
elements.sent-2411-185: Every value stored in this mode is concatenated with its LSM-generated sequence number (sn), so that snapshot queries can verify whether this value pertains to their snapshot.
elements.sent-2411-186: Note that even if all keys are stored in direct mode (i.e., each has a single version), the KVS is insufficient by itself because it does not support range scans.
elements.sent-2411-187: To this end, we use an LSM as an ordered secondary index for the data in the KVS.
elements.sent-2411-188: Consider, for a moment, the idealized scenario whereby keys always have at most one version.

elements.sent-2411-228: Because a Bloom filter may return false positives, a query that does search the SST file must still check the \code{vm}\ bit in the appropriate SST entry.
elements.sent-2411-229: Importantly, the Bloom filters are very compact ($\sim$10 bits per key) in traditional LSMs~\cite{DayanAI17 , and more so in KV-Tandem, where versioned entries are a small fraction.}

elements.sent-2411-354: XDP-Rocks is a RocksDB-compatible storage engine that implements the KV-Tandem design atop XDP~\cite{Dayan-XDP , a hardware-accelerated KVS technology, which we overview in Section~\ref{sec:xdp .}}
elements.sent-2411-355: XDP-Rocks addresses multiple practical challenges that are not addressed by KV-Tandem per se but are required in production; these are described in Section~\ref{sec:xdprocks .}
elements.sent-2411-356: XDP~\cite{Dayan-XDP is a storage SDK that provides a KVS API~\cite{KVSSD .}}
elements.sent-2411-357: The host library is a thin software layer on top of a storage processor PCIe card.
elements.sent-2411-358: XDP communicates with the storage directly, bypassing the filesystem, using proprietary data layout and index.
elements.sent-2411-359: The storage processor manages either directly-attached or over-fabric NVMe SSDs.
elements.sent-2411-360: It offloads index update, lookup, and data compression to FPGA circuitry.
elements.sent-2411-361: XDP also provides transparent RAID5 reliability.
elements.sent-2411-362: The XDP software model supports variable-size logical shards, or databases, to partition the storage space.
elements.sent-2411-363: Database creation and deletion are instant atomic operations.
elements.sent-2411-364: KVS operations (get, put, and delete) are provided at the database level.
elements.sent-2411-365: Additionally, XDP provides a full database unordered scan API for analytics and replication.
elements.sent-2411-366: XDP incorporates efficient GC (with just 7\% overprovisioning), which facilitates database deletion with zero extra I/O.
elements.sent-2411-367: The XDP KVS is essentially a hashtable atop log-structured storage.
elements.sent-2411-368: Its spirit is similar to Bitcask~\cite{Bitcask and Aerospike~\cite{SrinivasanBCSGI16 , but it scales better than them.}}
elements.sent-2411-369: The memory overhead is between 2.5 B and 3.5 B per key, depending on the expected value size -- an order-of-magnitude less than the closest competition~\cite{SrinivasanBCSGI16 .}
elements.sent-2411-370: This allows maintaining the whole index in DRAM, i.e., the key-to-address mapping incurs no extra I/O.
elements.sent-2411-371: XDP has no additional data cache in host DRAM; this is left to the application.
elements.sent-2411-372: The XDP write path aggregates random writes in a small (64 KB) host arrival buffer that is periodically flushed to a capacitor-backed onboard buffer.
elements.sent-2411-373: Upon reaching capacity, the latter is indexed, compressed, and flushed to SSD storage in big (1--2 GB) stripes.
elements.sent-2411-374: Every value is stored together with its hash key.
elements.sent-2411-375: Within each stripe, the data is ordered by database id and hash key, so the values within the same database are clustered per-stripe but not ordered by user key.
elements.sent-2411-376: Clustering by database id serves whole-database scans, which use sequential I/O.
elements.sent-2411-377: Intra-cluster ordering by hash key serves memory-efficient hashtable encoding.
elements.sent-2411-378: The hashtable is compressed -- it reduces 32-bit hash keys to small variable-length fingerprints.
elements.sent-2411-379: Its bits-per-key ratio is close to the minimum required for unique decoding.
elements.sent-2411-380: But compression comes at a cost -- updating a non-empty slot in the table requires testing if the colliding fingerprint maps to an existing key or a new one.
elements.sent-2411-381: Therefore, when flushing a stripe, XDP re-reads every colliding fingerprint to discover the full key.
elements.sent-2411-382: If that key is different from the new key, the whole slot is re-encoded.
elements.sent-2411-383: This background read is called fetch-existing-entry, or fee.
elements.sent-2411-384: To reduce the performance cost of fees, XDP's put and delete APIs provide an overwrite hint to indicate whether the update applies to an existing key.
elements.sent-2411-385: XDP endures host surprise power-down failures through a shutdown procedure that drains the buffered data to storage.
elements.sent-2411-386: It also guarantees data integrity in case of application crash through a recovery mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-387: The maximal data loss is limited to the host arrival buffer size.
elements.sent-2411-388: This section focuses on how XDP-Rocks addresses real-life challenges and compares its implementation details to RocksDB's.
elements.sent-2411-389: For efficient storage management, the LSM is stored in XDP via a custom KVS filesystem (KVFS), as described in Section \ref{sec:kvfs .}
elements.sent-2411-390: Scans are accelerated using parallel random-read workers, as explained in Section \ref{sec:xdprocks-scan .}
elements.sent-2411-391: We discuss the XDP-Rocks cache structure in Section \ref{sec:caching , and conclude with checkpoints and backup in Section \ref{sec:checkpoint .}}
elements.sent-2411-392: For lack of space we skip other implementation details, e.g., supporting the RocksDB's compaction filter API~\cite{CompactionFilter and tuning its rate limiter~\cite{RateLimiter for KV-separation.}}
elements.sent-2411-393: A significant system management challenge in KV-separated LSMs is allocating capacity to the LSM and the KVS over the same physical device(s).
elements.sent-2411-394: A straightforward approach could pre-partition the storage space between the LSM (keys) and the values in advance.
elements.sent-2411-395: But doing so without wasting storage space requires advance knowledge about the key-to-value size ratio statistics.
elements.sent-2411-396: Moreover, LSM space allocation must accommodate for temporary SA during compactions~\cite{DayanWDPBT22 .}
elements.sent-2411-397: XDP-Rocks instead uses XDP to store both the LSM and the values, without pre-partitioning.
elements.sent-2411-398: To facilitate data storage over XDP, XDP-Rocks implements KVFS -- a KVS filesystem.
elements.sent-2411-399: Every XDP-Rocks database maps to two distinct XDP databases -- one for values and one for LSM (SST and WAL) files.
elements.sent-2411-400: The latter uses RocksDB's virtual-filesystem mechanism~\cite{DongPPAESDPJKPZ23 , which maps the LSM directory to the XDP database.}
elements.sent-2411-401: File sizes are flexible, and every file holds metadata that links it to its XDP database.
elements.sent-2411-402: A database drop translates to deleting the two XDP databases and the top-level directory.
elements.sent-2411-403: KVFS is tailored to the LSM write-once-read-many I/O pattern.
elements.sent-2411-404: XDP-Rocks writes SST and WAL files to KVFS as sequences of approximately equal-sized logical blocks (4 KB for SST and 32 KB for WAL).
elements.sent-2411-405: Every read retrieves a sequence of one or more blocks.
elements.sent-2411-406: KVFS handles this workload by storing every file as a set of KV-pairs, each standing for a single block.
elements.sent-2411-407: The KV-pairs are organized in variable-size extents; a KVFS file is a single extent.
elements.sent-2411-408: The keys used by KVFS to store blocks in the same extent share a common prefix (extent id), and their suffix is a running index.
elements.sent-2411-409: Allocating new extent ids for new files comes with a cost -- it causes writes to use new keys for KVFS blocks, which triggers a background read-after-write in XDP (fee, see Section~\ref{sec:xdp ).}
elements.sent-2411-410: To forgo this cost, KVFS recycles extent keys.
elements.sent-2411-411: Every time a file is deleted, its extent id returns to the free pool, and whenever a new file is created, KVFS first tries to reuse an existing extent id.
elements.sent-2411-412: For the reused keys, KVFS applies an overwrite hint, which avoids the fee overhead.
elements.sent-2411-413: This boosts the KVFS write performance by approximately 20\% versus the na\"ive design.
elements.sent-2411-414: An extra challenge is ACID maintenance of the extent pool metadata, to address crash recovery.
elements.sent-2411-415: This is achieved through the RocksDB manifest (metadata log) mechanism~\cite{RocksDB .}
elements.sent-2411-416: Range reads (scans) pose the biggest challenge for XDP-Rocks due to the use of an unordered KVS.
elements.sent-2411-417: Although our target workload uses them sparingly, we still need to offer reasonable performance for those that do occur.
elements.sent-2411-418: Our solution is similar to WiscKey's~\cite{wiscKey -- expedite scans through parallel reads.}
elements.sent-2411-419: Namely, XDP-Rocks employs a background worker thread pool to read multiple values in parallel, coupled with a concurrent queue to handle out-of-order completions.
elements.sent-2411-420: The maximum number of workers is configurable per-iterator.
elements.sent-2411-421: In addition to value-read parallelism, XDP-Rocks uses the same mechanism to facilitate readahead in KVFS sequential reads.
elements.sent-2411-422: Namely, multiple threads (4 by default) prefetch sequential KVFS blocks in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-423: This pipelining reduces the number of stalls in application-level scans as well as in compactions and recovery.
elements.sent-2411-424: Note that these mechanisms cannot fully match the sequential read performance of baseline RocksDB, which relies on standard filesystem readahead (2 GB, by default), especially as the scans scale (Section~\ref{sec:microbench ).}
elements.sent-2411-425: Recall that XDP does not have a cache of its own (Section~\ref{sec:xdp ), so it is up to XDP-Rocks to cache hot values.}
elements.sent-2411-426: Like RocksDB, XDP-Rocks uses two types of caches.
elements.sent-2411-427: The first is a block cache holding LSM SST file blocks to speed up LSM searches.
elements.sent-2411-428: The second is a row cache that holds (variable-size) values rather than (fixed-size) SST data blocks.
elements.sent-2411-429: The latter supports random access with better memory efficiency, unless values are small, in which case the per-value overhead is dominant.
elements.sent-2411-430: The Bloom filters and the SST index blocks of all valid LSM files are permanently pinned in memory as part of the block cache in both systems.
elements.sent-2411-431: XDP-Rocks's row cache is more efficient than RocksDB's because RocksDB does not have direct storage of values by key, so its row cache holds popular values per SST file (the file id is part of the key).
elements.sent-2411-432: When a cached value becomes obsolete in case of update or deletion, its eviction from the row cache is lazy, to avoid explicit search of the affected file.
elements.sent-2411-433: In contrast, XDP-Rocks caches values under their user keys and updates them in-place.
elements.sent-2411-434: The RocksDB \code{checkpoint(dir) API in XDP-Rocks creates a persistent point-in-time view of an entire database in the directory \code{dir .}}
elements.sent-2411-435: The checkpoint can then be used, e.g., by backup tools, to replicate the database \cite{BackupRocksDB .}
elements.sent-2411-436: In XDP-Rocks, we restrict ourselves to read-only checkpoints, which are the most common use case; the primary replica remains read-write.
elements.sent-2411-437: An XDP-Rocks checkpoint is a database that stores a copy-on-write (CoW) clone of the primary replica's LSM tree under \code{dir and shares its KVS storage (values and KVFS) with the primary.}
elements.sent-2411-438: XDP-Rocks leverages the snapshot API to capture a virtual copy of the primary's storage at the time of checkpoint creation.
elements.sent-2411-439: Once a snapshot is created, updates to the primary do not overwrite values that pertain to the checkpoint.
elements.sent-2411-440: XDP-Rocks persists the snapshot's \code{sn timestamp, and re-installs that snapshot every time the primary database is reopened.}
elements.sent-2411-441: The checkpoint's deletion de-persists its \code{sn and removes the snapshot from the primary.}
elements.sent-2411-442: Note that RocksDB's checkpoints are simpler than XDP-Rocks's: they only use the CoW mechanism for LSM and do not require snapshots for retaining values (because RocksDB is not KV-separated).
elements.sent-2411-443: Long-lived checkpoints degrade performance because they cause all subsequent writes to the primary database to be versioned, which effectively disables the LSM bypass.
elements.sent-2411-444: They also waste space for keeping snapshot-protected values, albeit much less than RocksDB, which doubles storage through CoW as the primary LSM evolves.
elements.sent-2411-445: In principle, XDP-Rocks's backup could have been created via the standard RocksDB API, by scanning the whole checkpoint database and re-inserting its KV pairs into the initially empty target database one-by-one.
elements.sent-2411-446: However, this approach would have been inefficient, due to inherent scan performance challenges in KV-Tandem.
elements.sent-2411-447: Speeding up scans through internal parallel reads (Section~\ref{sec:xdprocks-scan ) offers a partial remedy but is ultimately inefficient for copying terabytes of data.}
elements.sent-2411-448: To support efficient backup, XDP-Rocks relies on file-level copy of the checkpoint LSM (as RocksDB does~\cite{BackupRocksDB ), coupled with efficient copy of the underlying KV-separated values.}
elements.sent-2411-449: Since checkpoints are read-only, the data can be copied in any order -- for instance, the order of values needs not match the order of their keys.
elements.sent-2411-450: The copy process streams the two datasets into two initially empty XDP databases (values and KVFS) at the target and complements them with a copy of \code{dir , the checkpoint directory atop KVFS.}
elements.sent-2411-451: At the end of this bottom-up construction, the target holds a consistent XDP-Rocks database.
elements.sent-2411-452: The LSM-copy uses KVFS whole-file scan, which is slower than RocksDB's (Section~\ref{sec:xdprocks-scan ) but still reasonably fast because the LSM is small.}
elements.sent-2411-453: The value-copy scans the KVS value database (which is shared with the primary) via XDP's whole-database out-of-order scan API that that exploits sequential I/O to its full extent (Section~\ref{sec:xdp ).}
elements.sent-2411-454: At the end of the copy process, the backup storage might hold newer writes that occurred to the primary after the snapshot.
elements.sent-2411-455: To trim the backup, we scan the primary's WAL starting from the snapshot \code{sn and delete the new writes from the backup's KVS, via the standard API.}

elements.sent-2411-585: We have introduced a new paradigm for key-value storage engines that combines LSM-based key storage alongside an optimized KVS holding values.
elements.sent-2411-586: At the heart of this paradigm lies the KV-Tandem algorithm, designed to maximize performance through its LSM bypass mechanism, which significantly reduces indirection overhead.
elements.sent-2411-587: We reified this approach by building a full-blown storage engine, XDP-Rocks, using Pliops XDP as the KVS and a RocksDB-like LSM.
elements.sent-2411-588: Extensive evaluations on production-grade workloads, covering both micro- and system-level benchmarks, demonstrated that XDP-Rocks delivers significant speedups over state-of-the-art solutions, particularly for random-read-and-write workloads at scale.
elements.sent-2411-589: KV-Tandem was tailored for typical NoSQL workloads that predominantly rely on simple random access but occasionally require range queries.
elements.sent-2411-590: Such patterns are common in consumer Internet applications, such as social networks~\cite{CaoDVD20 .}
elements.sent-2411-591: For example, one of our customers, a leading video-sharing platform, uses XDP-Rocks to support its internal key-value database.
elements.sent-2411-592: Random writes (e.g., new posts) and reads (e.g., post views) dominate, whereas scans (e.g., feed refreshes) are infrequent and short, often involving only a small number of records.
elements.sent-2411-593: However, it is important to recognize that KV-Tandem is not geared towards hybrid transactional-analytical processing (HTAP) workloads~\cite{HTAP, Taft2020, TiDB-20 , which use range queries often.}
elements.sent-2411-594: The unordered nature of the KVS backend limits sequential I/O, making XDP-Rocks suboptimal for scan-heavy workloads.
elements.sent-2411-595: While internal parallelism can mitigate this limitation by improving scan performance, especially in the presence of concurrent updates, this approach relies on multiple background workers.
elements.sent-2411-596: Such a strategy incurs additional overhead and lacks scalability for workloads with high scan frequency.
elements.sent-2411-597: Another limitation arises in scenarios where snapshots are frequently created and retired.
elements.sent-2411-598: KV-Tandem assumes that snapshots are relatively infrequent, enabling the direct mode to remain the dominant operational mode.
elements.sent-2411-599: A surge in snapshot activity, however, would shift the system towards versioned storage, negating the LSM bypass benefits.
elements.sent-2411-600: Frequent snapshot turnover could further exacerbate the problem by triggering mass renaming events.
elements.sent-2411-601: Each renaming involves a blocking read operation, which could slow down LSM compactions and degrade overall write performance~\cite{RateLimiter .}
elements.sent-2411-602: Adapting KV-Tandem to HTAP workloads is a valuable direction for future work.
elements.sent-2411-603: We identify several areas for enhancement:.
elements.sent-2411-604: Improved range query efficiency: Next-generation KVS implementations could be designed to exploit sequential read I/O (at least to some extent) without compromising random access performance.
elements.sent-2411-605: Techniques such as quotient filters~\cite{Bender2012 could be explored to enable faster range queries without relying solely on parallelism.}
elements.sent-2411-606: Enhanced snapshot handling: The rename penalty associated with frequent snapshots could be mitigated by extending the KVS model to include a copy-free rename API.
elements.sent-2411-607: Beyond this, adding MVCC capabilities directly at the KVS level could eliminate the reliance on the LSM for version management during random reads, allowing all snapshot reads to bypass the LSM entirely.
elements.sent-2411-608: The LSM would remain necessary only for range scans.
elements.sent-2411-609: Memory-efficient KVS-level MVCC: While some KVSs incorporate MVCC~\cite{ForestDB or use version-friendly data structures~\cite{Bender2012 , achieving this in a memory-efficient manner remains challenging.}}
elements.sent-2411-610: Incorporating MVCC into XDP’s hash table index -- while retaining its memory compactness -- would require addressing the compression difficulties associated with generic sequence numbers.
elements.sent-2411-611: We hope that future advancements will extend the KV-Tandem architecture to address these challenges, broadening its applicability to a wider range of workloads, including those requiring frequent analytics or high snapshot turnover.
elements.sent-2411-612: Consider, e.g., HTAP~\cite{HTAP databases like CockroachDB~\cite{Taft2020 and TiDB~\cite{TiDB-20 that combine reads and writes for transactions with scans for analytics.}}}
elements.sent-2411-613: KV-Tandem provides reasonable performance when scans are infrequent and even surpasses competition when they concur with updates (Section~\ref{sec:microbench ).}
elements.sent-2411-614: However, scan performance is achieved at the cost of large number of background random-read workers -- a wasteful and poorly-scaling approach.
elements.sent-2411-615: Since the data layout is controlled by the underlying KVS, it is desirable that next-generation KVS's support sequential reads at least to some extent without compromising the random reads.
elements.sent-2411-616: Random reads and writes also suffer from concurrent scans.
elements.sent-2411-617: For example, if at least one scan executes at any given time (i.e., there is always at least one active snapshot), then every concurrent write is stored in versioned form.
elements.sent-2411-618: This effectively disables LSM bypass, which impacts the random-read speed (Section~\ref{sec:microbench ).}
elements.sent-2411-619: Moreover, the turnover of active snapshots triggers background value renaming in KVS (from versioned to direct, Section~\ref{sec:kv-tandem ).}
elements.sent-2411-620: A rename, which happens to every value ever written, includes a blocking SSD read, therefore the background compactions slow down.
elements.sent-2411-621: An overflow of compaction jobs may trigger the rate limiter~\cite{RateLimiter , which in turn may back off the application write speed up to a complete halt.}
elements.sent-2411-622: In this context, applying KV-Tandem in full is counterproductive, and simpler designs like XDP-Rocks-nodirect may perform better.
elements.sent-2411-623: Future KVS designs might provide novel primitives to take on the above challenge -- e.g., a copy-free rename API.
elements.sent-2411-624: A more radical approach would be designing a KVS in which MVCC is a first-class citizen.
elements.sent-2411-625: In that case, all random reads (including from snapshots) would retrieve their data directly from the KVS, whereas the LSM would only serve range scans.
elements.sent-2411-626: The above approach, which is reminiscent of some prior work~\cite{ForestDB, WiredTiger , must compete with XDP's hashtable memory efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-627: That could prove challenging, since generic sequence numbers are not easily compressible.
elements.sent-2411-628: We defer those ideas to future research.. \bibliographystyle{alpha.}
